ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a mixed cheese and charcuterie sharing platter. FOODON_03315873 (cheese food product) is used as the closest parent category covering the dominant ingredient class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite platter category. A00LC (Cheese, generic) is used as the closest defensible parent given cheese is the primary component. Charcuterie and accompaniments are secondary.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226760005 (Cheese) is used as the closest parent. No SNOMED CT concept exists for a composite cheese and charcuterie platter.

main_ingredients: assorted cheeses (e.g. brie, cheddar, gouda, blue cheese)

serving.natural_language_portion: One sharing platter intended for 3 to 4 persons, estimated at approximately 600 g total (roughly 150–200 g per person), comprising assorted cheeses, cured meats, crackers, fruits, nuts, and condiments.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Cheese, assorted varieties (composite); Cured meat / charcuterie composite; Crackers; Fruits and nuts — weighted blend estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a composite cheese and charcuterie platter. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central entries for the typical components of such a platter: assorted cheeses (~45% by weight, USDA FDC NDB 01009 cheddar, 01026 brie, 01015 gouda as representative), cured meats/salami (~20%, USDA FDC 01054), plain crackers (~15%, USDA FDC 18228), fresh grapes and figs (~12%, USDA FDC 09132, 09089), mixed nuts (~5%, USDA FDC 12023), and honey/jam condiments (~3%, USDA FDC 19296). Proportions are based on typical café-style cheese platter composition. Values are per 100 g of the combined platter weight.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Cheese (GI ~0–15, effectively low); Crackers and mixed platter composite estimated at GI ~40.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cheese itself has a GI of approximately 0–15 (negligible glycemic response, University of Sydney GI Database). Cured meats have negligible GI. Crackers have GI approximately 65–70. Fruits (grapes) GI approximately 46–59. A weighted composite GI for this platter is estimated at approximately 40, reflecting the dominant low-GI cheese and meat components offset by moderate-GI crackers and fruits. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 111 g carbohydrate in 600 g platter) / 100 = 44.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from aged cheeses and cured meats
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of this platter, as the combination of aged cheeses and cured charcuterie contributes approximately 480 mg sodium per 100 g, which can significantly elevate daily sodium intake toward or beyond the 2,000 mg recommended limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from crackers and fruits with low overall GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: The platter has a low composite GI (~40) due to the dominant cheese and meat components, but the crackers and fruit portions contribute meaningful carbohydrates; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate cracker and fruit portions and pair with protein-rich cheese to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat and sodium from cheese and processed cured meats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This platter is high in saturated fat (~11.5 g per 100 g) and sodium (~480 mg per 100 g) from cheese and charcuterie; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption and favour lower-fat cheese varieties and reduce cured meat portions to manage LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A cheese and charcuterie sharing platter is a Western-style assembled cold platter designed for group dining, typically comprising an assortment of aged and soft cheeses (such as brie, cheddar, gouda, and blue cheese), cured and sliced meats (such as salami, prosciutto, and coppa), plain or seeded crackers, fresh and dried fruits (grapes, figs, dates), mixed nuts, and sweet condiments such as honey or fruit jam. In Singapore, this style of platter is commonly offered at café-style establishments and Western-influenced restaurants as a sharing starter or social snack. The platter served at EC Coffee Bar on Carpenter Street is marketed as the 'Love & Cheese Platter' and is sized for 3 to 4 persons. Nutritionally, the platter is energy-dense at approximately 310 kcal per 100 g, with high fat content (21 g per 100 g, predominantly saturated at 11.5 g) from the cheese and cured meats, moderate protein (12.5 g per 100 g), and moderate carbohydrate (18.5 g per 100 g) from crackers and fruits. Sodium content is elevated (~480 mg per 100 g) due to the salt-curing and aging processes used in both cheese and charcuterie production. The composite glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low), as cheese and cured meats contribute negligible glycemic load while crackers and fruits add moderate glycemic carbohydrates. Clinically, this platter warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and saturated fat density. For those with Type 2 Diabetes, the low GI is favourable but portion control of the cracker and fruit components is advised. The platter is not suitable for vegans, is not halal-certified (pork-derived charcuterie is typical), and contains common allergens including milk, gluten, and tree nuts.
common_user_queries: Love and Cheese Platter field survey
Is cheese platter good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific composite platter. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central entries for the typical components (cheeses, cured meats, crackers, fruits, nuts, condiments). The GI is an analogous composite estimate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the platter composition is well-characterised for this food category.
notes: 1. This record corresponds to the 'Love & Cheese Platter (for 3 to 4 Pax)' listed on field survey data by EC Coffee Bar - Carpenter Street (food_id GD003543). The canonical food name has been normalised to 'Cheese and Charcuterie Platter' for database consistency. 2. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central component entries; no single approved-source entry exists for this composite platter. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not individually confirmed from a single source entry; they are plausible composite estimates. 4. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record; all micronutrient fields have been estimated from component USDA data. 5. The cuisine tag 'Malaysian' from the input source reflects the restaurant's broader cuisine classification on field survey; the platter itself is Western in style and has been reclassified accordingly. 6. Multilingual names are provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the food is served in Singapore. 7. Halal status is flagged false as typical charcuterie contains pork-derived products; this should be verified against the specific restaurant's sourcing. 8. Price information from input (SGD 41.60) is not included in the RESULT template structure but is noted here for reference.
